retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Around 70% of the cases in which thyroglossal duct cyst are present are found during childhood, with a prevalence of intracystic thyroid tissue ranging from 5% to 62% of the cases, meaning that the prevalence of thyroglossal duct cyst carcinoma is very low.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, the failure for the thyroglossal duct to obliterate by the 10th week of gestation is a frequent cause for the apparition of midline neck masses, called thyroglossal duct cyst. Around 7% of the normal population will suffer from this condition. The location of these masses can appear anywhere along the traject of the thyroglossal duct, and can even result from partial obliteration of the duct. This, essentially, means that if at any point in the pathway, the duct did not obliterate itself, a possible cystic mass will appear.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Cysts -- Anatomical Pathology -- Thyroglossal duct cysts
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglossal duct cysts arise from remnants of the thyroglossal duct that form as the thyroid descends from the pharyngeal floor to the anterior neck. They present as a midline cystic nodule on the anterior neck that moves with swallowing and tongue protrusion; they can also present as lateral cystic nodules from foregut remnants. Children and young adults are most commonly affected. [56] The microscopic features include variable lining epithelium, which may be columnar (with or without cilia), cuboidal, or stratified squamous (see Image. Thyroglossal Duct Cyst). Thyroid follicles (ie, cuboidal cells surrounding amorphous pink material called thyroid colloid) are present in the surrounding tissue (see Image. Thyroglossal Cyst With Follicles).

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglossal duct cysts are fluid-filled structures lined with either respiratory epithelium, squamous epithelium, or a combination of both types of tissue. Due to their high susceptibility to infection, these cysts often show signs of inflammation, characterized by the presence of granulation tissue or giant cells. In approximately 70% of cases, small areas of ectopic thyroid gland tissue can be found, typically located within the wall of the cyst.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical features of thyroglossal duct cysts. Apply best practices for evaluating patients with thyroglossal duct cysts. Select the suitable surgical technique for excising thyroglossal duct cysts, considering patient characteristics, anatomy, and strategies to prevent recurrence. Implement collaboration among interprofessional team members to enhance outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients with thyroglossal duct cysts, while considering potential recurrence and complications. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Most cystic masses arise at the infrahyoid level (65% of the cases) and result from the secretion of the epithelial lining that remained from the non-obliterated duct, resulting in possible inflammation and, therefore, the formation of a thyroglossal duct cyst. These are the most common cause of midline neck masses around the world.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This course examines thyroglossal duct cysts, including neck embryology and anatomy, and accurate diagnosis, which is essential for effective treatment planning. This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence in identifying thyroglossal duct cysts, performing the recommended evaluation, and implementing an appropriate interprofessional approach when managing this condition to provide optimal patient care and minimize complications and recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: A thyroglossal duct cyst is the most common congenital cervical anomaly, affecting approximately 7% of the population. These cysts arise from the incomplete closure of the thyroglossal duct, an embryological structure formed during the thyroid gland’s descent from the base of the tongue to the lower neck. Thyroglossal duct cysts typically present as midline neck masses near the hyoid bone and can occur anywhere along the thyroid’s developmental path. While thyroglossal duct cysts are most commonly found in childhood, usually before age 10, they can also appear later in young adults as painless, mobile masses. These cysts may develop into sinuses and fistulas or become infected.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Thyroid -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Lingual Cyst - This is a thyroglossal duct cyst occurring at the base of the tongue. These can present with difficulty in swallowing or breathing. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Knowledge of neck embryology and anatomy aids in understanding how thyroglossal duct cysts develop. A thyroglossal duct cyst is an embryological remnant that forms due to the failure of the thyroglossal duct to close, which extends from the foramen cecum in the tongue to the thyroid's position in the neck. The thyroid begins to develop in the third week of gestation as a median outgrowth from the primitive pharynx. The thyroid primordium originates at the foramen cecum, located at the junction of the anterior two-thirds and posterior one-third of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The development of thyroglossal duct cyst, due to the failed obliteration of the thyroglossal duct before the tenth week of gestation, requires a surgical procedure with removal of the cyst/mass. This surgical procedure necessary for the removal of the cyst/mass is referred to as the Sistrunk procedure and is considered the preferred method of resection of these midline neck cysts and/or masses. Depending on whether the developed mass is benign or malignant, treatment should go accordingly. As previously mentioned, the Sistrunk procedure would be the treatment of choice in cystic masses, which have a recurrence rate of under 3% while applying this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Other masses may arise from the persistence of the thyroglossal duct, such as thyroglossal duct cyst carcinomas, which are more prevalent in women within the age range of 20 to 50 years old. The exact etiology behind these carcinomas is not well understood, and even though they are very well associated with various types of thyroid cancer, they account for less than 1% of the cases. The most common form of cancer associated with thyroglossal duct cyst carcinoma is the thyroid papillary carcinoma, which is present in 85% of the cases. Following the papillary thyroid carcinoma, histologically speaking, the most common cancers associated with thyroglossal duct cyst carcinoma are the squamous cell carcinoma, follicular cell carcinoma, anaplastic carcinoma, epidermoid carcinoma, and Hurthle cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: From this point onwards, the thyroid then descends to the neck, passing anteriorly and closely to the developing hyoid bone and thyroid cartilage, finally reaching its final position in the inferior pre-tracheal neck by the seventh week of gestation. The distal part of the thyroglossal duct differentiates into the pyramidal lobe of the thyroid gland in 50% of the cases, while the thyroid gland is forming itself. The rest of the duct is, supposedly, expected to obliterate by the tenth week of gestation. If any part of the thyroglossal duct persists after the tenth week of gestation, a rise in the probability of pathology occurs. The persistence of any portion of the thyroglossal duct becomes the gateway to the formation of the thyroglossal duct cyst. If given enough development to the pathology, it can even cause thyroglossal duct cyst carcinoma. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglossal duct cysts are the most common congenital cervical anomalies, with a prevalence of 7% in the population. These cysts arise from the incomplete closure of the thyroglossal duct, an embryological structure formed during the thyroid gland’s descent from the base of the tongue to the lower neck. Thyroglossal duct cysts typically present as midline neck masses near the hyoid bone and can occur anywhere along the thyroid’s developmental path. While thyroglossal duct cysts are most commonly found in childhood, usually before age 10, they can also appear later in young adults as painless, mobile masses. [1] Moreover, these cysts may develop into sinuses and fistulas or become infected.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As for the treatment and prognostics of these masses, given that the most common type of thyroglossal duct cyst carcinoma is the papillary thyroid cancer, the prognosis is good. Because the treatment of papillary thyroid cancer has improved over the years, patients tend to respond well to the usual therapy. As for other types of carcinoma, prognosis worsens as cancer becomes more aggressive, and thus, the treatment options become limited due to the poor response to chemotherapeutic and radiotherapeutic agents. [2] [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical relevance of the thyroglossal duct adheres to its embryologic function. The descent of the thyroid primordium begins at the base of the tongue, specifically at the foramen caecum, until it reaches its final resting place at the level of the 2nd and 3rd cartilage of the trachea, where the thyroid primordium will differentiate itself into the thyroid gland.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2551
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is categorized as an اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is anatomically associated with head and neck structures. This connection highlights that thyroid malignancies arise within the endocrine system and involve the anatomical region of the head and neck, which is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled growth of cancerous cells. The thyroid‑originating tumor that spreads to cervical lymph nodes illustrates how a primary malignant neoplasm can metastasize, while the primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus exemplifies a malignant proliferation arising directly within the thymic tissue. Both conditions underscore the importance of recognizing malignant neoplastic proliferations as pathological processes that require accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid, indicating that thyroid malignancies arise within this region. Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder), linking the disease to broader hormonal system pathology. The primary therapeutic intervention for thyroid cancer is thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a surgical procedure that is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (the Arabic term for thyroidectomy). This procedure is also recognized as the target anatomy–procedure relationship for the head and neck structure, reinforcing its role in the management of thyroid disease. The synonym relationship between thyroidectomy; partial or complete and استئصال الدرقية further clarifies that both terms refer to the same surgical treatment. Together, these connections underscore the anatomical, pathological, and therapeutic links among head and neck structures, thyroid cancer, endocrine disorders, and thyroidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: for diverse operative procedures ranging from endocrine gland removal and biliary duct exploration to cranial and urogenital tumor resections, while clearly distinguishing itself from certain spinal decompression surgeries that are not considered excisional interventions. This delineation aids clinicians in coding, planning, and communicating the scope of surgical care across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2173
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy), reflecting its categorization within endocrine pathologies and the common surgical intervention used in its treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is described with the morphology term “الورم السرطاني” and is directly mapped to the broader category of cancer of the pancreas. Metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck is classified as a type of “الورم السرطاني” (inverse isa) and its specific morphology is identified as “السرطان النقيلي”. The metastatic malignant neoplasm that involves lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid represents a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation and is characterized by the morphology “ورم خبيث أولي”. Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas also exhibits the morphology “ورم خبيث أولي”, is a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation, and is mapped to cancer of the pancreas. Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone shares the same primary malignant morphology “ورم خبيث أولي” and is likewise a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. The term “سرطانة توتية” is an inverse isa of “ورم خبيث أولي”, indicating it is a specific subtype of primary malignant tumor. Finally, primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus is identified as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. Together, these relationships clarify how various pancreatic, head‑and‑neck, thyroid‑derived, nasal‑bone, and thymic malignancies are categorized by their pathological processes and morphological descriptors, supporting accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management.
